source_intake_record.raw_question_text: It is believed that nucleons $(\mathrm{N})$ interact directly through the weak interaction and that the latter violates parity conservation. One way to study the nature of the $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}$ weak interaction is by means of $\alpha$-decay, as typified by the decays of the $3^{+}, T=1$ and $3^{-}, T=0$ states of ${ }^{20} \mathrm{Ne}$ (Fig. 2.29). In the following you will be asked to explain the principles of an experiment to measure the weak-interaction matrix element between these states, $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text {weak }}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$. Allowing for a parity-violating matrix element $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text {weak }}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$of 1 eV , estimate the $\alpha$ width of the parity-forbidden transition, $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (forbidden), in terms of the $\alpha$ width of the parity-allowed transition, $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (allowed). Assume $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (allowed) is small compared with the separation energy between the $3^{+}, 3^{-}$states.

the problem asks for a width estimate in terms of another width, implying at least a derivation step plus a numerical substitution step
question_text is long and physics-heavy, suggesting multi-condition reasoning rather than direct lookup

the target expression appears to be a single quantitative result, making rule-based or tolerance-based checking feasible
registry_passed=true and both question and answer are present, so the sample is structurally usable for evaluation

normalization_record.normalized_question_text: It is believed that nucleons $(\mathrm{N})$ interact directly through the weak interaction and that the latter violates parity conservation. One way to study the nature of the $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}$ weak interaction is by means of $\alpha$-decay, as typified by the decays of the $3^{+}, T=1$ and $3^{-}, T=0$ states of ${}^{20}\mathrm{Ne}$ (Fig. 2.29). In the following you will be asked to explain the principles of an experiment to measure the weak-interaction matrix element between these states, $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text{weak}}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$. Allowing for a parity-violating matrix element $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text{weak}}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$ of 1 eV, estimate the $\alpha$ width of the parity-forbidden transition, $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (forbidden), in terms of the $\alpha$ width of the parity-allowed transition, $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (allowed). Assume $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (allowed) is small compared with the separation energy between the $3^{+}, 3^{-}$ states.

metadata.reasoning_chain:
Fermi's golden rule gives the first order transition probability per unit time as
$$
\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{\hbar}\left|V_{f i}\right|^{2} \rho\left(E_{f}\right),
$$
where $V_{f i}$ is the transition matrix element and $\rho\left(E_{f}\right)$ is the final state density. Then the width of the parity-allowed transition $\left(3^{-}, T=0\right.$ to $\left.{ }^{16} O+\alpha\right)$ is
$$
\Gamma_{\alpha}=\frac{2 \pi}{\hbar}\left|V_{3^{-} \rightarrow{ }^{16} \mathrm{O}}\right|^{2} \rho\left(E_{f}\right) .
$$

The parity-forbidden transition $\left(3^{+}, T=1\right.$ to $\left.{ }^{16} \mathrm{O}+\alpha\right)$ is a second order process, for which
$$
\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{\hbar}\left|\sum_{n 
eq i} \frac{V_{f n} V_{n i}}{E_{i}-E_{n}+i \varepsilon}\right|^{2} \rho\left(E_{f}\right),
$$
where $2 \varepsilon$ is the width of the intermediate state, and the summation is to include all intermediate states. In this case, the only intermediate state is that with $3^{-}, T=0$. Hence
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma_{\alpha}^{\prime} & \left.=\frac{2 \pi}{\hbar}\left|V_{3^{-} \rightarrow{ }^{16} \mathrm{O}}\right|^{2} \frac{1}{\left(E_{i}-E_{n}\right)^{2}+\varepsilon^{2}}\left|\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text {weak }}\right| 3^{-}\right\rangle\left.\right|^{2} \rho\left(E_{f}\right) 
& =\Gamma_{\alpha} \frac{\left.\left|\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text {weak }}\right| 3^{-}\right\rangle\left.\right|^{2}}{(\Delta E)^{2}+\left(\Gamma_{\alpha} / 2\right)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $\Delta E$ is the energy spacing between the $3^{+}, 3^{-}$states, $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ is the width of the parity-allowed transition. If $\Gamma_{\alpha} \ll \Delta E$, as when $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text {weak }}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle=$ $1 \mathrm{eV}, \Delta E=0.052 \mathrm{MeV}=52 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{eV}$, we have
$$
\Gamma_{\alpha}^{\prime} \approx \frac{\left.\left|\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\mathrm{weak}}\right| 3^{-}\right\rangle\left.\right|^{2}}{(\Delta E)^{2}} \Gamma_{\alpha}=\frac{\Gamma_{\alpha}}{52^{2} \times 10^{6}}=3.7 \times 10^{-10} \Gamma_{\alpha} .
$$

node_records.canonical_value: It is believed that nucleons $(\mathrm{N})$ interact directly through the weak interaction and that the latter violates parity conservation. One way to study the nature of the $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}$ weak interaction is by means of $\alpha$-decay, as typified by the decays of the $3^{+}, T=1$ and $3^{-}, T=0$ states of ${}^{20}\mathrm{Ne}$ (Fig. 2.29). In the following you will be asked to explain the principles of an experiment to measure the weak-interaction matrix element between these states, $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text{weak}}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$. Allowing for a parity-violating matrix element $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text{weak}}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$ of 1 eV, estimate the $\alpha$ width of the parity-forbidden transition, $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (forbidden), in terms of the $\alpha$ width of the parity-allowed transition, $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (allowed). Assume $\Gamma_{\alpha}$ (allowed) is small compared with the separation energy between the $3^{+}, 3^{-}$ states.

agent_assessment.rationale: The problem statement is complete and specifies the target quantity, while the figure clearly provides the relevant level scheme and energy separation between the 3+ and 3- states needed to interpret the estimate. The expected answer format is a proportional alpha-width result, and the image-text pair is semantically coherent and usable.

cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is complete and semantically usable: the question and answer are materially present, the image-text pairing is clear enough to support the level-scheme reasoning, and rewrite/alignment/solvability evidence shows no serious failure. The noted image-path metadata anomaly does not undermine annotation usability.
